subject: Grace Hopper Submarine Cable
executive_summary:
Grace Hopper is a privately owned transatlantic submarine cable operated by Google, connecting New York (Bellport Cable Landing Station) to Bude, Cornwall (UK) and Bilbao, Spain (Sopelana Cable Landing Station). Named after pioneering computer scientist and US Navy Rear Admiral Grace Hopper, the cable spans 6,250 km to the UK and 6,300 km to Spain (approximately 7,191 km total including the branch), with 16 fiber pairs delivering 352 Tbps of total system capacity at 22 Tbps per pair. The cable entered service in September 2022 and was one of the first new cables connecting the US and UK since 2003.

Grace Hopper is strategically significant as part of Google's growing private submarine cable network, which now includes 16 subsea cable investments (Dunant, Equiano, Firmina, Curie, and consortium cables). Unlike consortium cables shared among multiple telcos, Google maintains sole ownership and control of Grace Hopper across US territory, UK territory, and international waters, with Telxius owning only the 12-nautical-mile extension from Spain's shore. This private ownership model gives Google complete control over capacity planning, routing, and security — a deliberate strategy to ensure quality of service for Google Cloud, Gmail, Meet, YouTube, and other services while reducing dependence on shared infrastructure.

The cable employs novel optical fiber switching technology — a world first for submarine cables — that enables dynamic rerouting of traffic between fiber pairs to improve resilience. In November 2024, Colt and Ciena completed the world's first terabit-class wavelength transmission across the Atlantic on Grace Hopper, achieving 1.2 Tbps per wavelength using WaveLogic 6 Extreme coherent optics. This positions the cable as a proving ground for next-generation transmission technology critical for AI-era bandwidth demands. While Grace Hopper benefits from the relative safety of its Atlantic routing (versus Red Sea cables), the broader submarine cable threat environment has worsened significantly with Russian hybrid warfare operations in European waters.

key_findings.headline: Google's Private Ownership Model Provides Strategic Control and Security Advantages
key_findings.detail: Google owns and maintains the entire Grace Hopper cable system across US territory, UK territory, and international waters. Only the 12-nautical-mile extension from Spain's shore is owned by Telxius (with Google holding usage rights). This private ownership model — replicated across Google's Dunant, Equiano, Firmina, and Curie cables — allows Google to proactively manage capacity, quality, latency, routing, technology, and scalability without depending on consortium negotiations. It also adds a security layer beyond what's available on shared infrastructure, as Google controls all access to the cable's management systems.

key_findings.headline: World's First Submarine Cable with Optical Fiber Switching Technology
key_findings.detail: Grace Hopper is the first submarine cable in the world to implement optical fiber switching, which enables dynamic rerouting of traffic between fiber pairs without manual intervention. This technology significantly improves resilience by allowing the system to automatically route around fiber pair failures, reducing the impact of partial cable damage. The innovation represents a generational advance in submarine cable reliability and has since been adopted in newer cable designs.

key_findings.headline: Terabit-Class Wavelength Transmission Achieved in Production Trial
key_findings.detail: In November 2024, Colt and Ciena completed the world's first terabit wavelength transmission across the Atlantic on Grace Hopper, achieving 1.2 Tbps per wavelength over 6,250+ km using Ciena's WaveLogic 6 Extreme (WL6e) coherent optics built on 3nm silicon. The trial doubled wavelength capacity while reducing power consumption by 50% compared to previous-generation technology, and increased spectral efficiency by approximately 15%. Colt plans to deploy WL6e in its production network for 400GbE and future 800GbE services.

key_findings.headline: Critical Infrastructure for Google Cloud's European Expansion
key_findings.detail: Grace Hopper's Spanish landing connects directly to the Derio Communications Hub near Bilbao, which tightly integrates with Google Cloud's Madrid region. The UK landing at Bude supports Google Cloud's London region and broader European operations. As Google Cloud competes with AWS and Azure for enterprise AI and cloud workloads, private cable infrastructure like Grace Hopper provides a competitive advantage in latency, capacity, and reliability that consortium cables cannot match. The cable is essential infrastructure for Google's European cloud revenue.

key_findings.headline: Part of Google's 16-Cable Global Network Creating Hyperscaler Infrastructure Dominance
key_findings.detail: Grace Hopper is one of 16 submarine cable investments by Google, including private cables (Dunant to France, Equiano to South Africa, Firmina to Argentina, Curie to Chile) and consortium cables (Echo, JGA, INDIGO, Havfrue). Together, these cables form a global private backbone that gives Google unprecedented control over international data routing. This trend of hyperscaler-owned submarine cables — replicated by Meta and Microsoft — represents a structural shift in global telecommunications from carrier-owned to tech company-owned infrastructure.

recent_developments.headline: World's First Terabit Transatlantic Wavelength Transmission on Grace Hopper
recent_developments.summary: Colt Technology Services and Ciena completed the world's first terabit-class wavelength transmission across the Atlantic Ocean on the Grace Hopper cable, achieving 1.2 Tbps per wavelength over 6,250+ km using Ciena's WaveLogic 6 Extreme coherent optics. The trial used 1.6 Tbps-capable 3nm silicon transceivers, doubling wavelength capacity and reducing power consumption by 50%. Colt announced plans to deploy WL6e across its production network to support AI content, gaming, and streaming customers.
recent_developments.impact: Validates next-generation coherent optics for transatlantic production use, positioning Grace Hopper as a testbed for technologies that will define AI-era submarine cable performance.

recent_developments.headline: Lumen Acquires Fiber Pair on Grace Hopper Cable
recent_developments.summary: Lumen Technologies, which serves as Grace Hopper's landing partner in the US and UK, acquired a fiber pair on the cable system. This gave Lumen direct capacity on one of the newest transatlantic routes, expanding its ability to serve enterprise customers with low-latency connectivity between North America and Europe. The deal demonstrated the commercial value of Google's private cable infrastructure to third-party carriers.
recent_developments.impact: Established a commercial capacity model for Google's private cables, allowing select partners to purchase fiber pairs while Google retains system control.

recent_developments.headline: Baltic Sea Cable Sabotage Raises Threat Level for All European Submarine Cables
recent_developments.summary: Seven submarine cables were cut in the Baltic Sea between November 2024 and January 2025 in suspected Russian hybrid warfare operations. The Eagle S tanker dragged its anchor for 62 miles on Christmas Day 2024, severing four cables. NATO launched Operation Baltic Sentry in response. While Grace Hopper was not targeted, the incidents prompted a comprehensive review of physical security for all submarine cables landing in the UK and Europe, including hardening of landing stations at Bude and Sopelana.
recent_developments.impact: Elevated the threat assessment for all European submarine cable infrastructure, including Grace Hopper's UK and Spanish landing points.

risk_assessment.current_risk: Moderate. Grace Hopper benefits from Google's private ownership (providing control over security), novel optical switching technology (improving resilience), and Atlantic routing (avoiding Red Sea chokepoints). However, the escalating threat of submarine cable sabotage in European waters — demonstrated by the Baltic Sea incidents — and the cable's strategic importance to Google Cloud create a non-trivial risk profile. The concentration of critical cloud infrastructure on a single cable between the US and UK/Spain represents a vulnerability if the cable is disrupted.
key_drivers: Russian hybrid warfare operations targeting submarine cables in European waters
Growing strategic importance of the cable for Google Cloud AI and enterprise workloads
Aging of alternative transatlantic cables increasing dependence on newer systems like Grace Hopper
Hyperscaler consolidation of submarine cable ownership reducing traditional carrier redundancy
UK landing at Bude making the cable subject to UK signals intelligence jurisdiction (GCHQ)
mitigation_actions: Maintain route diversity through Google's parallel Dunant cable (Virginia to France) and consortium cable investments
Deploy advanced cable monitoring systems (DAS/distributed acoustic sensing) for early detection of physical threats
Coordinate with UK and Spanish naval forces on cable protection, particularly near landing stations
Leverage optical fiber switching technology for automatic failover in case of partial cable damage
Continue advancing coherent optics technology to maximize capacity per fiber pair, reducing the number of cables needed
